with_agent_vs_baseline.pass_rate: +24% (100% vs 76%) — baseline finds the same data but uses its own JSON structure instead of the specified schema
with_agent_vs_baseline.tokens: +34% more tokens (25K vs 18K — agent reads instruction file and follows structured query plan)
with_agent_vs_baseline.quality: Agent produces schema-compliant JSON with consistent field names (ico, contracts[], flags[], summary{}). Baseline finds equivalent data but invents its own schema (supplier{}, contracts.items[], red_flags.by_type{}) making it incompatible with downstream agents. Both find the same flags and facts. Baseline actually adds richer analysis (key_concerns prose, mitigating_factors, funding_sources, IBAN) but at the cost of schema compatibility.

notes: Schema compliance is the discriminating factor: with_agent always matches the output spec, baseline always invents its own structure
Content assertions (flags, contracts, signatories, financial data) pass in both configurations — baseline is equally thorough at data gathering
Baseline DREVEX run was slowest (139s) despite fewer tokens — spent time on iterative queries and HTML parsing
Baseline adds value beyond spec: funding sources, IBAN, buyer breakdown, mitigating factors — useful but not schema-compatible
Both correctly handle missing data: null for unavailable phases, graceful finstat extraction
The calculates_summary assertion is discriminating: agent produces the exact summary format, baseline scatters stats across sections
DREVEX baseline incorrectly reports fs_tax_debtors as false (distinguishing DREVEX EU from other DREVEX entities), while agent correctly marks tax debtor as true
